beats.activity: Wake up, coffee, check weather and work messages.
beats.reasoning: Low energy from yesterday (40/100) means a slower, quieter morning. Mia checks the rain forecast and SR-520 closure, which will shape her commute strategy.

beats.activity: Drive to Interbay office, avoiding SR-520 via I-5 detour due to bridge closure.
beats.reasoning: SR-520 eastbound closed 9am–noon; Mia leaves early to avoid the worst congestion by taking I-5 south instead, adding ~15 min to her usual route.

beats.activity: Office work: listing calls, client emails, contract review for Wallingford condo offer.
beats.reasoning: Morning rain and low energy make staying focused at the office appealing; the young couple from yesterday's Wallingford showing likely needs follow-up.

beats.activity: Lunch at a nearby café; rainy day calls for soup and warm comfort food instead of market.
beats.reasoning: Rain is heaviest midday; Mia avoids the trek to Pike Place and grabs something warm and quick within walking distance of the office.

beats.activity: Back at office: follow-up emails, scheduling second showings.
beats.reasoning: Afternoon rain clearing by 2pm; Mia uses this time to lock in showings and paperwork before heading out.

beats.activity: Show two condos to returning clients in Capitol Hill.
beats.reasoning: Rain ending by 2pm; Mia heads into the city for afternoon showings while weather improves, staying productive on client commitments.

beats.activity: Drive back toward Kirkland, stopping for grocery errand.
beats.reasoning: Picking up ingredients on the way home; convenient stop in north Seattle before heading back to Kirkland.
Early return home due to low energy; Mia skips the Tuesday food bank shift to rest and prepare a good dinner instead.

beats.activity: Cook dinner using fresh ingredients; write letters.
beats.reasoning: Low energy day calls for quiet time; cooking and handwritten correspondence align with Mia's values and help her recharge.

diary:
Woke to that familiar grey Seattle drizzle—the kind that makes you want to stay under the covers with coffee. Of course the SR-520 closure meant dodging across I-5 this morning, watching the brake lights stack up like a parking lot. By the time I hit the office, my shoulders were already tense.

The day was steady, though. Fielded calls about that Wallingford condo—the offer's looking solid. Grabbed soup at the café around one; the rain made it feel right, you know? Warm bowl, quiet corner, watching people dash past outside.

The Capitol Hill showings went well enough. One couple seemed genuinely interested in the second place. Drive back was slow but meditative—stopped by Whole Foods, picked up some good produce. Cooked a real dinner at home instead of ordering in, wrote a couple letters I'd been putting off. There's something grounding about that.

Ended the day feeling oddly settled, despite the weather.
thought_process: The bridge closure rattled me initially—I factored in extra time and rerouted without fussing about it, but it set a slower pace for the whole morning. I chose comfort-first decisions all day: detour without complaint, soup instead of rushing, real cooking instead of convenience. The rain seemed to give me permission to slow down, which I needed. My mood shifted from that initial tension into something more reflective by evening. Work felt productive without being frantic, and I leaned into the domestic rituals at home. Maybe it's age, or maybe the weather just reminded me that not every Tuesday needs to be optimized—sometimes steady is enough.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
